title: Two markets in Bangkok, one street apart
summary: A short comparison of two markets in the same city that felt nothing alike.
topic: Two markets in Bangkok, one street apart
prompt: Write 8–10 sentences (150–250 words) comparing two markets, shops, or places in the same city or neighbourhood. Use comparatives and present simple. You may include a brief past anecdote.
hintStarters: There are two markets near my flat in…
The first one opens at five and the second one only opens at…
Both are loud, but the noise is completely different because…
sampleText: There are two markets near my flat in the Sai Yuan area of Bangkok, and they sit only one street apart. The first one, Talat Cha-am, opens at five in the morning and closes by ten. It is a small local market where older women sell fresh fish on metal trays, herbs in plastic bowls, and rice in paper cones. Most of the customers are neighbours, and the noise is the soft noise of regulars greeting each other. Prices are cheap, and nobody bargains. The second market, just behind the temple, only opens at four in the afternoon. It is louder, brighter, and three times more crowded. The smells come mostly from the food stalls — grilled pork, sticky rice, and pad thai cooked fast on huge metal pans. Almost everyone there is a tourist or a young office worker, and prices are usually double what they are at the morning market. Both are loud, but the noise is completely different. The morning one smells like the river. The evening one smells like smoke and sugar. I go to the first when I want to feel like a neighbour, and I go to the second when I want to feel like a visitor in my own city again.

sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: Có hai khu chợ gần căn hộ của tôi

sampleAnnotations.phrase: the noise is the soft noise of regulars greeting each other
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: tiếng ồn là tiếng ồn nhẹ của khách quen chào nhau

sampleAnnotations.phrase: grilled pork, sticky rice, and pad thai cooked fast on huge metal pans
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: thịt lợn nướng, xôi nếp, và pad thai nấu nhanh trên những chảo kim loại lớn

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Comparatives: '-er' vs 'more + adjective'
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: Tính từ ngắn dùng '-er' ('louder', 'brighter', 'cheaper'); tính từ dài hoặc kết thúc bằng -ed dùng 'more + adjective' ('more crowded'). 'Three times more crowded' = mức so sánh nhân lên.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: Short adjectives take '-er' ('louder', 'brighter', 'cheaper'); longer or -ed adjectives take 'more + adjective' ('more crowded'). 'Three times more crowded' = multiplied comparison.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Present simple for habitual contrast
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: Bài so sánh dùng present simple xuyên suốt ('opens', 'closes', 'sit', 'are', 'come') vì mô tả sự thật thường xuyên, không phải một sự kiện cụ thể.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: A comparison piece uses present simple throughout ('opens', 'closes', 'sit', 'are', 'come') because it describes habitual facts, not a single event.

sampleTranslationVi: Có hai khu chợ gần căn hộ của tôi ở khu Sai Yuan, Bangkok, và chúng cách nhau chỉ một con phố. Khu đầu tiên, Talat Cha-am, mở cửa lúc năm giờ sáng và đóng lúc mười giờ. Đó là một chợ nhỏ địa phương, nơi các bà lớn tuổi bán cá tươi trên khay kim loại, rau thơm trong chậu nhựa, và gạo trong giấy gói hình nón. Hầu hết khách là hàng xóm, và tiếng ồn là tiếng ồn nhẹ của khách quen chào nhau. Giá rẻ, và không ai mặc cả. Chợ thứ hai, ngay phía sau ngôi chùa, chỉ mở cửa lúc bốn giờ chiều. Nó ồn hơn, sáng hơn, và đông gấp ba lần. Mùi chủ yếu đến từ các sạp đồ ăn — thịt lợn nướng, xôi nếp, và pad thai nấu nhanh trên những chảo kim loại lớn. Hầu hết mọi người ở đó là khách du lịch hoặc nhân viên văn phòng trẻ, và giá thường gấp đôi giá ở chợ sáng. Cả hai đều ồn, nhưng tiếng ồn hoàn toàn khác nhau. Chợ sáng có mùi như mùi sông. Chợ tối có mùi khói và đường. Tôi đến chợ đầu khi muốn cảm thấy như một người hàng xóm, và tôi đến chợ thứ hai khi muốn cảm thấy như một du khách trong chính thành phố của mình lần nữa.

mcQuestions.prompt: Choose the correct present simple sentence.
options: Talat Cha-am open at five in the morning and close by ten.
Talat Cha-am is opening at five in the morning and closing by ten.
Talat Cha-am opens at five in the morning and closes by ten.
Talat Cha-am has opened at five in the morning and closes by ten.
mcQuestions.explanation: Present simple for habitual market hours; third person singular '-s'.

options: It is more loud, more bright, and three times more crowded.
It is louder, brighter, and three times crowded.
It is loud, bright, and three times more crowded.
It is louder, brighter, and three times more crowded.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'bargain' mean as a verb?
options: to discuss the price with the seller to make it lower
to buy something for free
to argue about politics
to leave a market quickly

mcQuestions.prompt: Choose the correct sentence about distance.
options: They sit only one street far.
They sit only one street apart.
They sit only one street between.
They sit only one street separately.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'X distance apart' = separated by X distance.

mcQuestions.prompt: Pick the correct sentence about majority customers.
options: Most of the customers is neighbours.
Most of customer are neighbours.
Most of the customers are neighbours.
The most of the customers are neighbours.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Most of + the + plural noun' + plural verb 'are'.

options: a customer who is polite
a customer who buys a lot
a customer from another city
a customer who comes often
Prices are usually double what they are at the morning market.
Prices are usually double than the morning market.
Prices are usually double of the morning market.
Prices are usually double from the morning market.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Double what + clause' is the natural comparative pattern in English.

mcQuestions.prompt: Pick the correct sentence with 'smell like'.
options: The morning one smells as the river.
The morning one smells like the river.
The morning one smell like the river.
The morning one smells of like the river.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Smell like + noun' is correct; third person singular '-s'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Choose the correct present simple negative.
options: Nobody bargain.
Nobody don't bargain.
Nobody bargains.
Nobody doesn't bargain.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Nobody' is third person singular and already negative; verb takes '-s'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Pick the natural sentence with 'feel like + noun'.
options: I go to the first when I want to feel like neighbour.
I go to the first when I want to feel as a neighbour.
I go to the first when I want to feel like to be a neighbour.
I go to the first when I want to feel like a neighbour.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Feel like + noun phrase' = experience the sensation of being that thing.

mcQuestions.prompt: Choose the correct sentence about parallel structure.
options: Both are loud, but the noise is completely different.
Both are loud, but the noise is completely differently.
Both is loud, but the noise is completely different.
Both are loud, but the noise are completely different.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Both' takes plural verb 'are'; 'the noise' (singular) takes 'is'; adjective 'different' modifies a noun via 'be'.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Why can two places in the same neighbourhood feel like different worlds? What actually makes a place feel local versus touristic?
